applied_optimizations.name: Vectorized Triton kernel with row-level parallelism
applied_optimizations.description: Each program handles one full output row (all width elements). Uses 2D grid (height_out, batch*channels) = (510, 512) = 261K programs. Vectorizes across width dimension using tl.arange(0, 512) for loads/stores. Eliminates inner width loop, reducing kernel launch overhead by ~500x compared to per-element grid. Achieves 4.7x speedup over PyTorch reference (0.347ms vs 1.63ms).

attempted_but_failed.name: 1D grid with one program per element
attempted_but_failed.reason: AMD GPU has grid x-dimension limit around 16-32M programs. With 133M elements, grid exceeded limit causing silent failures.
attempted_but_failed.error: Programs beyond ~16M grid index produced zero output

attempted_but_failed.name: 2D grid approach (first attempt)
attempted_but_failed.reason: Caused GPU memory access fault / core dump
attempted_but_failed.error: Memory access fault by GPU node-6

attempted_but_failed.name: Blocking with break statements
attempted_but_failed.reason: Triton does not support 'break' in loops
attempted_but_failed.error: UnsupportedLanguageConstruct: unsupported AST node type: Break

top_bottlenecks: Memory bandwidth - vectorized loads from 3 input rows per output row
Kernel computation - 3 multiply-adds per output element

notes: Problem 83: depthwise conv2d with asymmetric kernel (3, 1)
Key insight: vectorize across width dimension to reduce grid size and kernel launch overhead
BLOCK_WIDTH=512 matches input width, uses power-of-2 for efficient vectorization
Each program processes 512 elements with 3 loads + 3 multiplies + 3 adds + 1 store

attempted_but_failed.name: trial_4
